observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Give preferential attention on the titling of the 22 parcels of land and secure
other documents evidencing its ownership in order to establish absolute
ownership and to protect the interest of the Municipality. Exert extra effort
to secure the needed documents required to facilitate transfer of ownership
in the name of the Municipality;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the nine titled parcels of land
with assessed value of P106,780.00 based on appraisal value; and
c. Direct the Appraisal Committee to appraise the untitled lots to its current
market value and provide the same to the Municipal Accountant for
reference in recording in the books of accounts and upon securing the
Certificate of Title.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to capitalize the interest paid, fire safety evaluation clearance and
other expenses that are attributable to the Construction of the Three-Storey
Multi-Purpose Building in order to comply with IPSAS requirements and to
present fairly the balances of the affected accounts. Further, disclose in the
Notes to Financial Statements the accounting policy adapted and other
borrowing costs.
The proposed journal entry is as follows:
CIP-Buildings P2,244,358.21
Prior Period Adjustment P2,244,358.21

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the GSO-
Designate to prepare Property Transfer Report approved by the LCE and
furnish a copy to the Municipal Accountant in order for him to derecognize
the PPE items in the books of accounts. Moreover, we recommended that the
LCE instruct the Municipal Agriculturist and Agricultural Technologists
assigned at the different barangays to monitor the status of these transferred
properties. Also, the Municipal Accountant should see to it that these
transferred properties are properly recorded in their books and inventory of
the recipient barangays and should be covered by Property Acknowledgment
Receipt (PAR) in order to establish accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize procured supplies
and materials as Inventory and record as expenses only upon issuance as
supported by the RIS and Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI)
prepared by the Supply Custodian.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GSO-Designate
to register and ensure all serviceable motor vehicles of the Municipality with
the LTO and GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of payment of
damages for third party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents. Also,
undertake immediate marking of all vehicles/equipment with the phrase
“FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY” and the corresponding name of the agency on
each side of the vehicle to prevent unnecessary and unofficial use of
government motor vehicles.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GSO-Designate to
prepare and submit to the Auditor the Inventory Report including pictures on
the salvaged/scrapped materials from the demolished structures and through
the Appraisal and Disposal Committee determine its fair market value and sell
them at public auction to the highest bidder. The GSO-Designate and the
Municipal Engineering Office should coordinate to be able to prepare the said
report for the Accounting Office to make the proper accounting and disposal
of said materials. In addition, it is the responsibility of the Agency Head to see
to it that the salvaged materials be properly recorded, disposed and accounted
pursuant to provisions of Sections 102 and 105 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management collect the full amount of the Liquidated
Damages from the Contractors concerned by sending them letter of demand
indicating the schedule of payment copy furnish the Auditor. In case said
Contractors were not able to pay the amount on the date agreed on the
schedule of payment, the Municipality may refer to the procedures for the suspension or
blacklisting of the Contractor in accordance with the guidelines issued by the GPPB. Further, strictly impose Liquidated Damages against
delaying contractors pursuant to Sections 8.1 and 8.3 of Annex A of the 2016
Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that Management incorporate a separate caption in the
Annual LDRRMFIP, the PPAs of unexpended balances of prior years in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 in
order to fully utilize its use for the immediate realization of the objectives of
the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management program.

We recommended that Management submit on the first working day of
January of every year, the list of all on-going government projects and those
that are to be implemented during the year in order for the Auditor to conduct
his review and validation. Further, the Auditor should be notified of posting
of project signboards within ten (10) days after award of the project.

We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist comply with the above
requirements as required under Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-
386 dated October 20, 1992 in order for her to be relieved from accountability.
Further we recommended the recognition of the 23 heads goat in the books of
the Municipality.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
ensure that RIS and SSMI and other pertinent documents were attached to
the Journal Entry Voucher in recording the issuances of supplies and
materials and that all accounting entries affecting financial accounts should be
supported with adequate documentation.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to submit complete supporting schedules for all accounts presented in the financial statements to substantiate the validity and correctness of the account balances reported in the financial statements at year-end pursuant to Sections 111 and 114 of PD No. 1445 and provide breakdown on the beginning balances labeled as forwarded balances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive issue an order/memorandum immediately for the conduct of physical inventory taking within a determined period of time and submit the inventory report within ten (10) days from the completion of the inventory taking as required under Chapter 7, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs. A full time permanent personnel be employed who will be in-charge of property records considering the properties of the municipality is increasing every year. Also, monitor the progress of the inventory taking to ensure the submission of prescribed and complete RPCPPE within the target period. Lost/missing issued government properties should be charge to the persons accountable.

Further, we recommended that:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
-Maintain a complete Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards to support the details and breakdown and must be kept updated to facilitate reconciliation of accounting records with the Inventory Report; and
-Drop unserviceable properties from the books to ensure the correctness and accuracy of the Property, Plant and Equipment  accounts.
Require the Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer-Designate to:
-Maintain Property Cards to monitor the receipts and issuances of Property, Plant and Equipment. The property records should be reconciled against the  accounting records;
-For those PPEs found to be unserviceable, report them in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and dispose  in accordance with COA rules and regulations
-Classify the Inventory Report for each type of the Property, Plant and Equipment to facilitate reconciliation with the accounting records;
-Update/renew Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) on equipment/properties issued to various municipal officials and employees every after three (3) years of issue. Replace the previously issued Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) with Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) to comply with certain COA rules and regulations; and
-All properties of the municipality must have a property tag or sticker pasted on each item for proper identification.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a Certified List of Real Property Taxpayers with the corresponding amount of RPT/SET due and collectible during  year as basis in setting up the correct RPT/SET Receivables by the Municipal Accountant. The Municipal Accountant make proper coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and secure copies of the accomplished identified taxpayer cards as basis in posting the RPT/SET Receivable accounts to ensure that the respective accounts are accurately presented in the financial statements. Further, the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to arrive at a reliable and correct balance of the accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere on the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System specifically COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 in order to have a fair presentation of the account Infrastructure Assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately submit to the COA Auditor all disbursement vouchers for CY 2018 totaling P58,627,365.10 pursuant to the provisions of Section 7.2.1 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA) as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006  and Section 5.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 7, 1997 thus, giving the Auditor enough time to examine and audit all transactions and  request for inspection and evaluation by a COA Technical Audit Specialist on completed infrastructure projects.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the balances of said accounts to determine the actual  liability to the concerned agencies and prepare the appropriate adjustments.

We recommended that before granting a cash advance to any official and employee, a certification from the Municipal Accountant that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books and to be attached to the disbursement voucher as required under Section 1.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 in order to avoid the accumulation of the balances of cash advances during the year or at year-end.

We recommended that management cause the submission of the lacking documents to support the claim in order to avoid suspension in audit. Also, submit accomplishment report to COA General Counsel through the Audit Team Leader as required in the Legal Retainer Review.

We recommended that the Municipality require contractors the submission of material test results before the conduct of inspection by agency personnel and payment of claims for infrastructure projects so that management will be assured of the quality of materials used.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant examine the completeness of the supporting documents for every transactions of the Municipality before approving the same. Further, recommended that require suppliers of goods issue delivery receipts and sales invoice and Inspection and Acceptance Report should be properly accomplish in order to validate the actual receipt and delivery of the items procured as basis in the imposition of penalty in case, there is delay of delivery by the supplier.

We recommended that Construction Safety and Health Program be integrated  in the computation of construction cost of all projects to be implemented by the municipality in order to ensure the protection and welfare of construction workers as well as the general public. Further,  we recommended that the BAC  require the submission of duly approved Construction Safety and Health Program from the winning bidders/contractors as one of the documents that form part of the contracts on infrastructure projects to ensure compliance with R.A. 9184 and issuances of the DOLE.

We recommended that agency officials concerned require contractors post  warranty security, one year after the completion of the project, for the protection of the interest of the municipality in case of  structural defects and failures that may arise. The amount shall be released only after the lapse of the applicable warranty periods pursuant to the above regulations. Likewise, the Municipal Engineer being in-charge in the implementation of  infrastructure projects of the municipality should monitor compliance by contractors of this requirements in conformity with Section 62.2.3 of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that retention money be deducted from the contract price or a special bank guarantee deposit be issued by the supplier to cover  the obligation for any manufacturing defects of the items purchased and to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that the municipality observe the minimum employee’s monthly net take home pay prescribed under Section 48 of the General Appropriations Act of FY 2018 in order that the efficiency and effectiveness of employees in the performance of  their duties and functions could not be affected.

We recommended that the municipality refrain from paying registration fees directly to the hotel venue and require official receipts from sponsoring agency/ies  for complete documentation of government expenditures and to avoid suspensions in audit.

We recommended that the municipality formulate a written policy guidelines in assessing the necessity of hiring job order employees taking into consideration the reasonable number needed by the different offices in order to avoid excessive workforce, idle manhours and work duplication.

We recommended that the municipality fully implement the programs/projects/activities under the 20% Development Fund in order to attain the agency’s goals and objectives for the year as stated in the Municipal Development Plan and also to deliver the timely expected benefits due to beneficiaries/constituents.

We recommended that the municipality through the LDRRMO fully implement the LDRRMF programs and activities and see to it that all expenses incurred were in accordance with the approve plan and budget. Further, strictly monitor the implementation of the LDRRMF programs, projects and activities to ensure that the plan was fully implemented.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point Person to fully implement the GAD Plan and Budget in order not to deprive constituents of the gender responsive services. Further, recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that all expenses incurred were in accordance with the approved plan and budget to avoid the issuance of notice of suspensions and/or disallowances.

We recommended that the Municipality implement all the programs and projects funded under  RA 7171 and RA 8240 to attain the intended purpose of the fund and maximum benefits derived from the program.
